items.q: What is melisma in word setting?
items.correct: Several notes sung to one syllable of text, often used to highlight an expressive word.
items.misconception: Melisma means many syllables sung quickly on a single repeated note.
items.random_wrong: Melisma is the gap left between two verses of a song.
items.why: Students reverse the definition: the ratio is many notes to one syllable. Recognising which words a composer extends melismatically reveals their expressive priorities.

items.q: Should a composition include as many different ideas as possible?
items.correct: No - strong pieces develop a small number of ideas thoroughly, creating unity as well as contrast.
items.misconception: More new ideas always make a composition more interesting.
items.random_wrong: A composition may legally contain only one idea.
items.why: A stream of unrelated ideas sounds incoherent; assessment criteria reward development and balance of unity with contrast, not sheer quantity of material.

items.q: Do cadences only occur at the very end of a piece?
items.correct: No - cadences punctuate the ends of phrases throughout, like commas and full stops in prose.
items.misconception: A cadence is only the final two chords of the whole composition.
items.random_wrong: Cadences appear only in vocal music.
items.why: Treating cadences as endings-only leaves phrases unpunctuated; tonal composing relies on regular cadence points to define keys and give the listener structural signposts.

items.q: Several notes sung to a single syllable of text is called what?
items.why: It's Melismatic setting. “Syllabic setting” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An ostinato is best described as what?
items.why: It's A persistently repeated pattern. “A sudden key change” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which chords are the primary triads in a major key?
items.why: It's I, IV and V. “II, III and VI” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A balanced classical-style melody often pairs an antecedent phrase with what?
items.correct: A consequent phrase of matching length
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A consequent phrase of matching length. “A drum fill” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An antecedent-consequent melody made of two four-bar phrases lasts how many bars in total?
items.why: Exact answer: 8 bars. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
